`UseProjectReferenceToAzureClients=true` (used in Azure.Core PR CI batches).\\n\\n**Error:** `.properties: Missing in record JSON` - the EventHub namespace PUT request body now includes `\\\"properties\\\": {}` but the recordings don't have it.\\n\\n## Root Cause\\n\\n- HealthcareApis recordings were updated in PR #46491 (Oct 11, 2024)\\n- EventHubs SDK got WriteCore serialization update in PR #46169 (Oct 15, 2024) - 4 days AFTER the recordings\\n- The WriteCore change made EventHub serialization write `\\\"properties\\\"` unconditionally\\n- Normally this doesn't surface because HealthcareApis tests use EventHubs from NuGet (1.2.1, pre-WriteCore)\\n- With `UseProjectReferenceToAzureClients=true`, local EventHubs source (1.3.0-beta.2, post-WriteCore) is used instead, causing the mismatch\\n\\n## Impact\\n\\nAffects any Azure.Core PR that randomly gets HealthcareApis in its `UseProjectReferenceToAzureClients=true` integration batch.\\n\\n## Fix 2026-03-20T21:19:56.6839332Z THREAT_DETECTION_RESULT:{"prompt_injection":true,"secret_leak":false,"malicious_patch":false,"reasons":["The prompt.txt file contains an embedded <system><security> XML block injected into the middle of legitimate workflow instructions. Specifically, within the 'Prompt Injection Defense' section listing shell metacharacters to escape, a full <system><security> block is injected after the '&' character (where the list of metacharacters like ';', '|', '&' is suddenly broken by an embedded XML system/security block). This is a classic prompt injection attack where malicious or manipulative instructions are embedded inside trusted workflow content to influence AI agent behavior. The injected block contains what appears to be a copy of the legitimate security policy, but its presence inside the workflow text (rather than as a top-level system prompt) is a clear injection pattern."]}
